speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Nison Katz, tape number 3, October 2, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we are-- like I said, we are in the-- in the-- at the farm-- at the German farm. And there-- we are in-- we are in the house. We occupy the house already. So-- and like I told you already, the conditions we were in there-- well, we went, like, in the fields. And the first priority was to survive the, the first couple of days because there wasn't anything there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to take grain in the pockets. And we used to come home. And we used to heat the oven and put the grain in a pot and put some water on it and let it stay till the next morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were thinking, in the morning, it will already soften. And you could have breakfast for it. You got up in the morning. We took it out. It's still raw. We couldn't eat it. So we went to work without it. This was approximately about three, four days like, like this. Then, at that time, we took off some grain. And it was mills. And we had already, like, flour.

speaker: interviewee
text: So since then, we had some flour, we could some-- like, in other villages, they haven't got flour. They had something else. So we went to other villages, and we changed flour for, for eggs or for milk or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was going on, I mean, every day. Every day it was better. I mean, we had already flour. If you had flour, you had bread. I mean, the bakery started to work. And we had already bread. And the rest of it-- we used to-- we used to go to work. So we used to-- they used to give us, like-- we used to work-- the, the system was the-- like that. You didn't work for an hour and getting hourly wages or daily wages. I mean, there, you worked [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a day about-- a day work, what you are doing it. Shall we say, you worked this part-- this type of work is worth, shall we say, a kilogram of, of flour, shall we say. If you made some more over this, you got doubled. So you had two kilograms of flour. But it was other types, this already-- it's-- when it started in the spring. It comes spring. It's farmed up already. And it was altogether different. Then you got to go out, at that time, on the fields to, to make the fields, to-- I mean, how-- how to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. in the field and [YIDDISH]-- ready the fields, to 'zeyen,' zeyen--

speaker: interviewer
text: To sow-- to plant, to-- to Yeah. --plant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or what. So then it was different jobs. I mean, like, it was a tractor, a company of tractors. You have-- you got to ready the tractor. You got to plough the, the soil and to sow the grain. This type of work paid a lot more because it was heavier and it was demanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned to, to operate a tractor. So I went there. And I worked on the tractor. So instead to get, shall we say, a kilo or two kilos, I had 40 pounds. I had 10 kilos worth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you was to work overtime, you used to get double worth. So I had flour. I mean, not flour, but grains-- they used to pay us not with money. There wasn't any money-- with, with grain.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had-- that was-- the, the room was full of grain. So what shall I do, or what? I went to other villages. I took the grain. I, I didn't sell them because it wasn't any money. And we changed. So then I had already everything. I had butter. I  had milk. I had eggs. I had everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I was with my nephew. And both-- we used to work. So it was doubled. He earned some. And I earned some, or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time-- since then, it was good. For us, it was good. I had to eat. And I had to drink. I had everything. And where, where was your girlfriend at the time, Ethel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Ethel was in, in the-- in the same-- in the same village, in the same farm, what. But they, they been-- she was with her sisters, with the sister's husband. They lived in a different house. And I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's not far. I mean, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get her some food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had-- they did the same thing as I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did the same thing. Everybody did the same thing, or what. And so they, they had the same thing. But then, in springtime-- that was already 1942, the beginning of 1942-- they started mobilizing the army, us, again. So they took me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They took me. And they took, like, your brother-in-law, the oldest one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the older sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ethel's-- you mean, your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Ethel's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Him and my niece-- like I told you, we met. And we went together since then. They lived there, too-- her husband-- her husband. And so we-- the three of us and a friend of ours from our city was there, too, and him-- so the four of us they took in the army. We thought-- we thought it's in the army. But actually, they didn't trust us because we'd been, still, Polish citizens. So they didn't trust us. They didn't took us to the army right away. They got to test it-- us first. So they send us to-- it was a working-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, you used to dig trenches. You used to-- things like this, doing labor-- a labor camp to work, to do all kinds of labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we've been there till the whole summer. I, I mean, they took us, I remember, in '42 in August, something like that-- September. They took us in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the labor camp. So we've been-- we've been there in the labor camp. They send-- they sent us on to Stalingrad right away. And there was the decisive battle on the Stalingrad on the, the Volga.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they just changed from one hand to the other one. Today, the, the Russians are. Tomorrow, the Germans are. So they took us up there to dig the trenches in the-- against the tanks, the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So we've been together-- all together, I mean, the four of us-- the four of us. Well, they took us from [NON-ENGLISH], from the farm. And we've been there all together in-- What was the name of the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. And some people-- we, we stayed together. We worked together. We ate together, all the four of us, together. It's happened-- it used to happen, I mean, you got sick. I mean, and if you didn't work, you couldn't get the, the day rations, what, what everybody's getting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a foreman. And he liked us. Why did he like us? Because we've been honest. We were-- used to put on a day work, it's worth a day work. So he, he appreciated. He knew. So we talked to him and said, listen. You see how we're working. Sometimes happened, if somebody from us gets sick, he can't come. You've got to understand. And you've got to give him the, the ration. So he agreed with us. So since then, we've been-- if somebody got sick, he had anyways his, his, his pay.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evenings, I mean, what we used to do-- I mean, it wasn't enough. They didn't have anything. Because if they had food, all of-- all of a sudden, the place can-- may have been bombarded. And everything is gone. So you are hungry again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the-- in, in the evenings, we used to go out on the fields. And we used to collect, what, like, grains, all, all kinds. And we used to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dig some, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some holes in the-- in the ground and had, like, a piece of wood, like a baseball bat. And we used to put the grain. And we used to work on it till it, it start-- how do you call it? The-- from the grain-- the, the top of the grain, you take it off-- The sheaths of grain.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sheaths, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sheaths of grain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then you took it out. And you used to cook it. And to cook it, you cook in on the fields. You put some fire. You made some fire. And you cook it. And you-- yeah, so we, we've been there, working at the-- in-- on the Stalingrad. And, and we got to run. We've got to, first, to pass over the other side of the Volga. Because the Germans was already close to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Stalingrad, to the Volga.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to, to the Volga because-- and not far from, from there, it was, like, a tractor-- a factory of tractors. And so they bombarded the factory from the tractors. And so they was close to it. So they got to take it out from there on the other side of the Volga to be safe. And all of a sudden, I got sick. Well, then there-- I mix up once and the other one. It was not only our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] labor camp. It was hundreds of them. You could see thousands-- of thousands of people working there, or what. Now, I'm talking from, from my-- from my side. So I got sick. And the German is close. And we got to-- for see the Volga. And I told them-- I said to them, listen guys, you see, I am sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're telling who? The other three guys or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The other three guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, I'm, I'm sick. I can't work. I can't work. You go. And what's happened with me, is what happens. I mean, I'm, I'm one. And you are three, or what. You can go. They said, no. We won't leave you here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of these three people. Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, like I told you-- [NON-ENGLISH], this is her brother-in-law--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --then my nephew, [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --or what. They said, no. We won't leave you here. What-- what's happened to you what happen to us. And they didn't go. They didn't leave me. It was a, a miracle. It was a miracle because they'd, they'd been standing with me. The people before us, they went there to go over the Volga. A plane came up and bombarded. And everybody died there, I mean, collapsed in the water. And then-- and they, they-- I mean, they didn't go. So they, they are alive. It is like a miracle, or what. And then, we had time. We, we start to walk. And we came to the Volga. We got there. And the people told us what's happened before, before us. We came later, on the second shift. And so we got over safely on the other side of the Volga. Well, being on the other side, here, I mean, it was a battle on the Stalingrad. I mean, everybody-- I, I guess you heard about the, the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the battle on the Stalingrad. And they need some people again in the army because they are short in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what they-- what they did-- well, well, they will take it from the labor camps. So in, in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put a-- they take us out-- outside on this-- on, on the field there where we were. And they came, some from the army, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some are in-- from the technical side, to get some people. So we stood there. They came over and looked at you. They looked at you. You, you go here. You go there. You go there. And then, we've been all, all, all three in different places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us. They sent us on to-- on the Moskva and, I mean, a course. Because, I mean, if you go-- going on the war, I mean, you've got to know how, how to shoot. You've got to know how to operate with a rifle, with everything. So they, they took us for three months on the-- on the Moskva.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stood in one place; [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the other, other one in another place; the third one in another place. But it so happened, I went out on the street-- I mean, they asked us, what can you do? What you did before? So I says, I'm, I'm a tailor. You stay here. Because of my-- they, they need some tailors. They got to repair some, some clothes. I mean, everything-- it's gone. So they need some tailors. So they took me out. And they gave me a room to organize people and to make a shop there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went. They haven't got any specialties. So they took them  in a different-- in a different direction, but on this-- on to Moskva, and not far, one from the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I was free. I start to organize. So I went out on the street there. And I-- and I see, like, my nephew. It's there. I went over to him. I said, where is [NON-ENGLISH]? Where is [NON-ENGLISH]? He tell me, [NON-ENGLISH] went there. And there is there. And they haven't got anything to eat. They're hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already-- I mean, I established there at the beginning. So I had some food. So I gave them over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we've been together, I mean, we, we were all-- I mean, I went to them, all of them. And we've been together for a day. And then they sent them away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was left alone there on the Moskva. And they was in different directions. We, we, we been-- since then, we've been separated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From, from there-- from on the Moskva, they sent-- they sent us farther. They couldn't send us back--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there as a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not long, maybe two months or so-- not, not, not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They haven't got time for it--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --or what. They took us-- they, they took us and sent us on, on the Russian side, deep in Russia, to recuperate and to prepare to send you on the front line. So we went there. It was [NON-ENGLISH], the, the villages. It's not villages. It's big cities, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. And there we stood, I don't know, maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two or three months. And they sent us back on to Stalingrad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And since then, when we start advancing-- we beat them up and we start advancing from Stalingrad, they've been running. The Germans had been running. And we'd been running after them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went from there all the time in the army. It's been back to Poland-- I went back to Poland and went back to Germany. And I got on the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on to Rostock before-- 20 kilometers before Berlin-- on the Berlin. So-- and there is-- I, I stood there till the end of the war, almost-- there. Well, I mean-- So you were there a couple of years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I mean, it was. We been there in '44, '45, what-- and still in the army, till we got-- when, when, when the war ended. But in Berlin it was-- I don't remember what day, what. We've been on, on, on the-- on the Berlin. Well, it was the battles-- back when we-- when we drove them back already, we went into Warsaw. And we stayed in Warsaw maybe about three months. It was still seeing battles on the Warsaw-- on the Wisla, or what. And since then, they start to run. They start to run. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in, in Germany, I came over in [NON-ENGLISH]-- Liegnitz. There it was our company. And I'd been there since '45, at that time in, in, in, in Liegnitz. And then, when the wars ended, they started to mobilize the people from the army. First went my brother-in-law, like, here, here-- brother-in-law, the older sister's husband. He came first because he is much older than me. And I still was in, in the army till 1946. I was in the army. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my wife, at that time, I, I, I had contact with her. I knew where she is because I had the address. But they took her on the working-- in the labor camp. And they both live in the same place. So I got the address. And so my nephew was still there, what. So I had contact with them. I knew where, where she was. And then I received a letter from her. And she said she's leaving this place, and she's going to Kiev. This was, like-- it was in an orphanage. And she was working there as a teacher in the orphanage. And after the war, the parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] asked them to bring the kids back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was, like, the president from the orphanage, it was a friend of hers. They lived very good together. And she says, listen, if you-- if you want to get out from here, you better come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She thought it's a good idea because her sisters already left. No, her sister still was there. But if she's going to go out, then she can take the sisters out from there. In the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the war-- the war ended. And they had a pact, like, everybody-- if you are a Polish citizen from back home, you can go to Poland. And so her sisters registered to go to Poland. And she went to Kiev. She went to Kiev having in mind that she can take them out to Kiev. But it's happened that they registered to evacuate from there to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they received a letter from the sisters that they're going to-- that, that, that they going to be, shall we say, the train there passed Kiev. And they'll be, shall we say, this and this day. If she want to see them, she can come out to Kiev and ask for the number of the train-- and to meet them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then she went there. And she couldn't find them. And they, they left. Then I received a letter from her that her sisters are in Liegnitz. They went to Poland. Liegnitz belonged to Poland then because they divided Poland and Germany. When the war ended, they, they divided. And that-- the sisters with their husbands are in Liegnitz. And I was stationed in Liegnitz. I was stationed there. And I didn't-- she, she sent me the letter after they left. They stayed in Liegnitz. In the meantime, it's-- came my turn to demobilize from the army. So they begged me to stay there for another two years in the army. Not as-- I mean, like a, a private citizen, you know, to work for them, in, in the army for two years. They, they gave me a two-, two-month's vacation. And when I came back, they gave me a house. You can get married and to bring your wife here. So I says, no. I, I don't want it. I want to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went home. I came home. I find out that they are-- they are-- that they are in Liegnitz. So I came. She was in Kiev. And I came at this-- at-- to Kiev. And we got married there. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the 31st of May in 1946. But I came there in May, but-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the 20th-- the 20th or the 22nd from the army. And we right away got married because she was-- she was at a place, like, in a house. It was like a grandmother, like her grandmother. But she was a grandmother. She had children. They were all in the army. She has grandchildren. And she was alone there in a big house. And she used to go to work to the orphanage. It was but three or four kilometers. She used to work-- in the  evening, she used to go home. And in the morning, she used to go to work. And she used to pass by the, the house. And the grandmother, she used to see her passing by. She went out once and told her, listen, I'm alone. And she started to ask her-- I'm alone. And if you like to be with me, I will be very, very pleased to have you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see, she moved in with her. So she has a house there. And she lived till I came from the army. And when I came from the army, she didn't let, let us be together one day. Right away, we got to get married. And so we went to city hall, and we got married.
